title: From ABAP Unit to Modern Testing at Scale
description: Bridges SAP's testing practices (ABAP Unit, manual functional testing, transport-based regression testing) to FAANG's testing culture. Covers the test pyramid, contract testing, property-based testing, chaos engineering, testing in production, and the cultural shift from 'testing before go-live' to 'continuous testing in CI/CD.'

sections.title: The Testing Culture Gap — From Go-Live Testing to Continuous Testing

description.value:
SAP's testing culture is driven by project timelines and go-live events. Testing happens in dedicated phases: unit testing (by developers), functional testing (by functional consultants), integration testing (by the project team), user acceptance testing (by business users), and regression testing (before upgrades). Each phase is manual, time-boxed, and occurs before a major milestone. FAANG's testing culture is continuous: tests run automatically with every code change, failures are detected within minutes, and the entire test suite runs multiple times per day.

The fundamental differences: (1) SAP: Testing is a phase. You test before go-live, then you stop testing until the next go-live. FAANG: Testing is a practice. You test with every change, continuously, forever. (2) SAP: Testing is manual. Functional consultants execute test scripts in the QA system. FAANG: Testing is automated. Code changes trigger CI pipelines that run unit, integration, and contract tests without human intervention. (3) SAP: Testing is expensive (dedicated test teams, test environments, test data management). FAANG: Testing is cheap (automated, cloud-based, self-service). (4) SAP: Testing is about preventing bad changes from reaching production. FAANG: Testing is about enabling rapid, safe changes to reach production.

What SAP engineers bring: (1) Domain knowledge: you understand complex business processes (order-to-cash, procure-to-pay) and can design effective end-to-end tests. (2) Compliance experience: you understand testing in regulated environments (SOX, GxP, GAAP). (3) Regression testing experience: you have experience with the pain of manual regression testing and understand why automated testing is valuable. The skill is translating this experience into FAANG's automated testing framework.

sections.title: ABAP Unit to Modern Unit Testing

description.value:
ABAP Unit (CL_AUNIT_ASSERT) is a mature unit testing framework with test classes, test methods, fixtures (setup/teardown), and assertions. It supports dependency injection through test doubles (CL_ABAP_TESTDOUBLE). SAP engineers who use ABAP Unit already understand the principles of unit testing: isolate the unit under test, mock dependencies, and assert expected outcomes. The transition to FAANG unit testing frameworks (JUnit, pytest, Jest, Go testing) is primarily about syntax and tooling, not concepts.

ABAP Unit concepts that translate directly: (1) Test class = test class/suite. Group related tests. (2) SETUP method = beforeEach/setUp. Initialize test data before each test. (3) TEARDOWN method = afterEach/tearDown. Clean up after each test. (4) CL_AUNIT_ASSERT->ASSERT_EQUALS = assertEquals/assertEqual. Compare expected vs actual values. (5) CL_ABAP_TESTDOUBLE = mock/stub. Simulate dependency behavior. (6) Test injection (IF_TEST_TOOL) = dependency injection. Replace real dependencies with test doubles.

What FAANG adds: (1) Property-based testing: Instead of writing specific assertions, describe the properties your code should satisfy. The framework generates random inputs and verifies the properties hold. Useful for data processing functions, validation logic, and state machines. SAP equivalent: no equivalent in ABAP Unit. (2) Mutation testing: The framework makes small changes (mutations) to your code and checks if the tests catch them. If tests pass after a mutation, the tests are not covering that code path. SAP equivalent: no equivalent. (3) Code coverage enforcement: CI blocks PRs that reduce coverage below a threshold. SAP equivalent: manual code review.

sections.codeBlock:
# ABAP Unit Test (SAP)
CLASS ltcl_pricing_calculation DEFINITION FOR TESTING.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS: calculate_discount FOR TESTING.
    DATA: mo_cut TYPE REF TO ycl_pricing_calculation.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ltcl_pricing_calculation IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD setup.
    CREATE OBJECT mo_cut.
  ENDMETHOD.
  
  METHOD calculate_discount.
    DATA: lv_result TYPE p.
    lv_result = mo_cut->calculate_discount(
      iv_amount = 1000
      iv_customer_type = 'PREMIUM'
    ).
    cl_aunit_assert=>assert_equals(
      act = lv_result
      exp = 200  " 20% discount for premium
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

# FAANG Equivalent (Python/pytest)
def test_calculate_discount_premium():
    calc = PricingCalculation()
    result = calc.calculate_discount(1000, 'PREMIUM')
    assert result == 200  # 20% discount for premium

# With property-based testing (Hypothesis)
from hypothesis import given, strategies as st

@given(
    amount=st.floats(min_value=0, max_value=100000),
    customer_type=st.sampled_from(['STANDARD', 'PREMIUM', 'VIP'])
)
def test_discount_never_exceeds_amount(amount, customer_type):
    calc = PricingCalculation()
    discount = calc.calculate_discount(amount, customer_type)
    assert discount <= amount  # Discount can never exceed the amount

sections.title: Contract Testing — From IDoc Validation to Consumer-Driven Contracts

description.value:
SAP's IDoc validation checks that an incoming IDoc matches the expected structure and data types. The sender and receiver agree on an IDoc type (e.g., ORDERS05), and the receiver validates the structure before processing. This is a form of contract testing. FAANG uses consumer-driven contract testing (Pact) to verify that service APIs meet consumer expectations.

Contract testing concepts: (1) Consumer-driven contracts: The consumer of an API writes a contract specifying what they expect from the provider (which endpoints, request format, response format, error handling). The provider runs the consumer's contract tests in their CI pipeline. If a provider change breaks a consumer contract, CI fails — before the change reaches production. (2) Provider verification: The provider runs all consumer contracts before deployment. If any contract fails, the provider fix or coordinates with the consumer before deploying. (3) IDoc to contract mapping: IDoc type = API contract (OpenAPI/Swagger). IDoc segment structure = request/response schema. IDoc mandatory fields = required request parameters. IDoc optional fields = optional request parameters. IDoc validation = contract test.

Why this matters for SAP engineers: You have experience with the pain of breaking integrations. An IDoc change that breaks a receiver process is exactly the same problem as a REST API change that breaks a mobile app. Contract testing addresses this at the CI/CD stage rather than after deployment. Bridge: 'In SAP, we validated IDoc structures at the receiver to prevent processing errors. In a FAANG context, I would apply the same principle using consumer-driven contract testing — each API consumer defines their expectations, and the provider verifies those expectations in CI before deployment. This catches breaking changes before they impact production.'

sections.title: Testing in Production — Verification Beyond Staging

description.value:
SAP testing philosophy: test in QA, never test in production. FAANG testing philosophy: test everywhere, including production — but safely. Testing in production includes techniques that are unfamiliar to SAP engineers but essential for reliable distributed systems.

Production testing techniques: (1) Canary deployments: Deploy the new version to a small subset of users (1% of traffic). Monitor error rates, latency, and business metrics. If healthy, roll out to more users. If unhealthy, roll back automatically. This is testing in production with a safety net. (2) Feature flags: Deploy code that is turned off by a feature flag. Enable the flag for internal users first, then a percentage of external users, then all users. If a problem is detected, disable the flag — no rollback needed. (3) Shadow traffic: Send a copy of production traffic to the new version without serving the results. Compare the new version's output to the old version's output. Detect regressions before any user sees them. (4) Chaos engineering: Intentionally inject failures into the production system (kill a service, introduce latency, exhaust resources) to verify that the system handles failures gracefully. This is the opposite of SAP's 'prevent all failures' approach — it's 'verify the system survives failures.'

SAP engineers' valuable perspective: You have seen the consequences of inadequate production testing — go-live incidents, post-migration issues, upgrade failures. Your experience with these pain points gives you insight into why FAANG invests heavily in production testing. Bridge: 'I have experienced production incidents after SAP go-lives and upgrades. I understand that you cannot fully simulate production in a staging environment. FAANG's approach of canary deployments, feature flags, and chaos engineering provides a safer way to validate changes in production — I would apply these techniques to minimize the risk of production incidents.'

sections.title: Chaos Engineering — Breaking Things on Purpose

description.value:
Chaos engineering is the practice of intentionally injecting failures into a system to verify its resilience. It was pioneered by Netflix (Chaos Monkey) and is now standard practice at FAANG. The philosophy: 'If we don't break our system on purpose, it will break at the worst possible time.'

Chaos engineering principles: (1) Start with a hypothesis: 'If the payment service becomes unavailable, the checkout service should gracefully degrade by displaying a message and queuing the order for later processing.' (2) Inject real failures: Kill the payment service process. Block network traffic to the payment service. Introduce latency to the payment service API calls. (3) Measure the impact: Does the checkout service degrade gracefully? Is the error rate within acceptable limits? Are users notified? Does the system recover when the payment service returns? (4) Fix what breaks: If the checkout service crashes when the payment service is unavailable, you have discovered a resilience gap. Fix it. (5) Automate the experiment: Run chaos experiments regularly (daily, weekly) as automated tests in the production or staging environment.

SAP vs FAANG failure philosophy: SAP systems are designed to prevent failures. High availability clusters, redundant hardware, backup systems — the goal is to prevent any component from failing. FAANG systems are designed assuming components will fail. Circuit breakers, bulkheads, retries, fallbacks, graceful degradation — the goal is to survive failures when they happen (and they will). SAP engineers must shift from 'how do I prevent failure?' to 'how do I design for failure?' Bridge: 'In SAP, we designed for failure prevention through redundancy and high availability. In a FAANG context, I would design for failure survival through circuit breakers, bulkheads, and graceful degradation — accepting that failures will happen and designing the system to handle them gracefully.'

details: Staff+ Insight: SAP engineers often underestimate their testing knowledge because they come from a culture where testing is manual and undervalued. The reality: you have tested complex enterprise systems with real business data, real users, and real regulatory constraints. You have experience with the pain of manual regression testing, the cost of production incidents, and the complexity of testing integrated systems. These experiences give you a deep appreciation for why FAANG invests so heavily in automated testing, contract testing, and production verification. The key is translating your testing philosophy (not your specific tools) into the FAANG context: 'I believe testing should be automated, continuous, and integrated into the development process. I believe testing should verify both correctness and resilience. I believe testing in production is necessary but must be done safely through canaries and feature flags.' This is a FAANG-compatible testing philosophy — just expressed in SAP terms.
